note: Street interviews conducted for MIT data-viz class. Fields summarize each person's statements; `quote` is verbatim where present. Some place names marked (?) were unclear in audio. `themes` are author-assigned tags intended for semantic placement against the globe's subtopics.

interviews.would_live: Braintree — trying to relocate so their second house can be rented out
interviews.quote: housing used to be cheaper outside of Boston central area
interviews.notes: One parent does electrical construction work. Another works in food service(?) at Northeastern; another sells at a fish market.

interviews.lives: Squantum(?) — "I'm surrounded by water, and I have a kayak in the backyard"
interviews.would_live: Back Bay — "historically beautiful. Lots of walking, lots of… open space"
interviews.commute: Ferry to Downtown — "half an hour of pure delight"
interviews.change: More reliable train service; roads that don't delay buses
interviews.quote: half an hour of pure delight
interviews.notes: Values waterfront living and walkability; critical of bus-delaying roads.

interviews.lives: Where she was born and raised (exact location unclear) — says it "has changed a lot" and is "not the same"
interviews.change: "I would love to have a self driving car and would never need to parallel park"
interviews.quote: I would love to have a self driving car and would never need to parallel park
interviews.notes: Car-oriented; neighborhood change is salient to her.

interviews.work: Transit ambassador (assigned a different station daily)
interviews.commute: Drives to whichever station she's assigned to; buses to stations she can't park at; has to pay for parking at some
interviews.notes: Works for the MBTA; rotates station assignments.

interviews.lives: Rockland(?) / Lynn(?) — "Rock Lynn", half a mile from the station; likes it because it's a small town
interviews.work: Currently in Chelsea next to a high school; every job is different
interviews.commute: T + commuter rail + electric scooter; ~2 hours each way. Takes scooter so he doesn't pay for parking
interviews.change: More frequent trains — "right now it's once an hour. Could cut 2 hours off the commute time if there were more"
interviews.notes: Long multi-modal commute; scooter used to avoid parking fees.

interviews.work: Construction; different sites; ~16 miles avg; ~45 min to Cambridge
interviews.commute: Bus + train; if driving, goes to Dorchester then takes the train
interviews.change: Sunday service — "no public transit runs from where he lives in Weymouth to station so [he has] to take an Uber"
interviews.notes: Service gaps force Uber use on Sundays.

interviews.would_live: San Francisco for family; in Boston would choose Salem (has previously lived in Braintree, Southie, Beacon Hill, Dorchester)
interviews.work: Pastry kitchens in Back Bay and Seaport
interviews.change: More accessible, more frequent schedule, service available past 3am
interviews.notes: Wants late-night transit; has lived in many Boston neighborhoods.

interviews.commute: Commuter rail; ~1 hour to 1 hour 20 min
interviews.change: Shorter commute; lower parking fees; free transit; more consistent during bad weather
interviews.notes: Parents work in Quincy and Downtown; one mention of Northeastern, BPL, United Airlines.

interviews.role: Practice assistant at Brigham & Women's Hospital
interviews.lives: Bedford — "originally I lived in New York, and then I moved here… I was able to find a cheaper apartment"
interviews.commute: "I usually catch the 4:25 AM train from my stop, and I get here by 6:00 AM" — ~1h50, 50 miles
interviews.quote: I usually catch the 4:25 AM train from my stop, and I get here by 6:00 AM
interviews.notes: Very early-morning rail commute; moved for housing affordability.

interviews.commute: ~25 min by commuter rail; if he misses it, bus + Red Line (~35 min). Last direct rail is 9:20am — "after those times, I have to, like, stop at [unintelligible station] or Quincy"
interviews.change: Faster, more consistent commuter rail times; through-running instead of ending in the morning — "the red line… they usually take a long time before they depart from the station. I don't understand why"
interviews.notes: Red Line dwell times and truncated commuter rail schedule are his main gripes.

interviews.role: Deliverer of medical equipment / preparer of medical information for Boston Scientific
interviews.lives: Dorchester — "getting to work… there's more surrounding things that I can get to faster… it feels like a central area"
interviews.commute: Walks to the commuter rail, ~20 min by rail; happy with it — "save money, save gas… don't worry about no car accidents"
interviews.change: How the train operates in inclement weather — delays force him home later
interviews.quote: save money, save gas… don't worry about no car accidents
interviews.notes: Happy commuter; appreciates centrality of Dorchester.

interviews.role: Teacher at Boston Public High Schools
interviews.lives: Dorchester — bought through the city's first-time home buyer program; used to rent in Brighton until COVID
interviews.would_live: South Boston or West Roxbury — closer to work
interviews.change: More commuter rail access — his family is at the end of the line (Braintree) and the commuter rail skips over
interviews.notes: Wife is pregnant and uses transit; he picks her up because station stairs are hard. Uses transit mainly to visit family.

interviews.lives: Middleborough — "peaceful, in the country, and has good neighbors"
interviews.change: Commuter rail reliability — "The app tells on itself, it says it's less than 75% on time"
interviews.quote: The app tells on itself, it says it's less than 75% on time

interviews.notes: Brief interview; generally satisfied. Also mentions Harvard.

interviews.role: CTO at a startup (also a data scientist colleague)
interviews.lives: San Francisco (moved from Seattle to start a company)
interviews.would_live: Seattle — "best of everything… national parks within 2 hours… skiing in the winter"
interviews.work: CTO; his colleague is a data scientist who works from home
interviews.commute: ~30 min between two MUNI buses in SF
interviews.change: More frequent trains, better connectivity so he doesn't have to make a connection
interviews.notes: Visiting; outside the Boston region. Useful as a comparison voice.

interviews.would_live: Cambridge — to "get rid of one car" and have more walkable amenities; likes the "vibrancy" and that residential blocks sit next to commercial ones
interviews.commute: None for work. ~10–15 min by car to errands. Nearest T is Tufts/Medford, 1.5 miles away
interviews.change: More reliable express buses — COVID paused the nearest express route near her house and it never came back; routes were changed after
interviews.quote: my life is not focused in downtown
interviews.notes: Prefers local independent stores; drives downtown for events.

interviews.role: Industrial pump rentals — contracts & logistics (desk job)
interviews.would_live: Somewhere in the city proper with a city-based job, so he can access public transit outside of work
interviews.work: New Jersey branch — moves equipment between branch locations
interviews.commute: 30 min by car to NJ at highway speeds; often ends up staying 9–10 hours for overtime; gets home 6–7pm; "Parking's a disaster"
interviews.change: More consistent transit funding — "funding in Pennsylvania, in particular, is state [run]. The city actually really doesn't have much say. Since it's run by Republicans, a lot of times they're very hesitant to give Philadelphia the money they need"
interviews.notes: Outside Boston; political framing of transit funding is the distinctive note.

interviews.lives: Somerville — "somewhere greener, quieter" (used to live in Boston's North End, which was "fun, although loud and busy")
interviews.commute: 12 min by bicycle. In fresh snow has to Uber or take the T — walks 15 min to Porter then takes the train
interviews.change: Safer, separated bike paths (more of them); make housing affordable — on a ~$70k post-doc salary he can't live alone in a one-bedroom without going "beyond Alewife or Newton or Watertown"
interviews.quote: You take the bridge across, and you can immediately tell that you are in Boston. Your life is in danger.
interviews.notes: Strong contrast between Cambridge/Somerville bike infrastructure and Boston proper; uses Blue Bikes occasionally but bikes his own road bike most places.
